item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is a lone embedded agent able to subdue everyone in a NY City precinct building?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt the same way, they are humans, we must not forget that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why would somebody say "lockdown repeat lockdown" in a text message? Why would they repeat themself? Back in this time period, aren't these messages trying to be as concise as possible? I guess it shows the significance of the threat, but it seems extremely unrealistic to me.

Also, too much in this story was left for us to figure out. It gave me a headache trying to understand what was going on, and this made the ending very unsatisfying, because all of that thinking and implication lead to no real conclusion.

Well written, but maybe change up the idea of the SCP and the foundation's knowledge on it, to make it more conclusive. -1.

what the fuck did i just read?
So, lemme get what happened straight. Police officers arrest people, and take some to the building. People inside the building fight with the agent over the phone call and probably kill him. Agent fighting with people at the arrest location orders some kind of warhead strike because the people are actually anomalous gypsie aliens. The remaining people at the precinct get killed/eaten, and then the alien guys flee to the police building, kill/eat wveryone in there, and are now building something or doing something from within the building.
Edit: Looked at discussion and now I understand what happened

A long-overdue benchmark! This is still one of my favorite format screws on the site because it doesn’t attempt to make the screw itself the story - it uses the screw to enhance the telling of the actual story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This line caught my attention as well. Almost feels like it would work on its own as a kind of three word horror story. This SCP is underrated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The formatting of this post adds to the overall immersion and helps to distinguish it from other entries on this website. The story is interesting maybe not the most amazing thing ever but at the same time it's clear you put a lot of thought into this. I really appreciate the creativity of the people posting on this site and hope to see more interesting ideas from you in particular in the future!

+1

item.title: Re: Unofficial Comment Feature #1/??
item.content:encoded:
I just recently reread this (I read it ages ago and never upvoted because I wasn't logged in), and yeah — I have to say, there's a lot of subtle freakiness here that I really like.

It's almost too subtle. I feel as if the low-rating of this article is reflective of its refusal to rely on cheap shots to grab the reader's attention or direct them to where the horror is happening. I feel as if this is both to the article's detriment and to its credit.

At the very least, there's a lot of stuff going on here that I suspect I could learn from. Hence, definitely a +1.

item.title: Unofficial Comment Feature #1/??
item.content:encoded:
By now, it's almost a platitude to say that SCPs are a story, but if they are, then not many authors on the site are good at pacing and structuring their SCPs in novel and inventive ways. Lost Precinct, however, is one of the few articles that I can say might actually be unique in the way that it approaches its story; the closest thing to it might be the 'bait-and-switch collapsible' style of SCP-1500, but it's considerably more sophisticated than that.

+ Rambling/Analysis/Wankery
– hide block

SCP-2409 has a number of things that I really really love about Michael Atreus' work in general. If you've read Ketergrams you understand the author knows his shit about clinical tone, and this skip is written with the same level of technical literacy that tale was written with. I love the Experimental Containment Research Group, I love the little detail about an 'ectoentropic leak' that stops paint from drying properly and in general I really adore the Special Containment Procedures. This is how you write hook containment procedures, kids, pay attention, and the foreshadowing and buildup present early on in the article is a major part of why this works from a structural standpoint.

Of course, where this article really shines is the teletype logs, and for good reason. Returning to the point about structure, you'll notice that this article uses the logs to build up on the threat levels that the Special Containment Procedures built up. Something inside the precinct was big and bad enough that the Foundation used something too anomalous to use anywhere else to keep it in there, and the slow reveal of just what that thing is over the course of the teletype logs is really well-paced.

Saying that 'things are scary because they're not shown' is kind of misleading advice, at least to me; a better way to phrase it is might be 'things are scary because we only see the parts we need to.' Hippo's horror work is a good example of that, with this being another. There are small disconcerting details about the threat that get built up here: "UNINJURED AND ABUSING REMAINS", "BUZZING AND ABNORMAL JERKING", "SWEAT IS WRONG COLOR", and the sting of the juxtaposition of "BEWARE PELVIC ANOMALY" with "INJ[E]CT OR INFL[A]T[E] VICS TO B[U]RST W APPNDGE / DEV[OU]R REMA[I]NS W SAME".

The fridge horror of the final message and the fact that 'Bronagh' is a female name that psul pointed out upthread only makes the mental picture presented even more horrific. And then after you realise that, you realise that they're stuck in there until 2023, with a threat that the Foundation wasn't able to put down without the aid of technology that broke the fundamental tenets of their organisation. And then after that you realise that the Foundation still hasn't come up with a way to deal with these things once they inevitably break out.

In horror, you never make the situation better for your characters. What Michael Atreus did with 2409 is create a situation in which we as the readers are subject to that same constant descent that good horror articles create: the feeling of a situation growing worse and worse, from the sterile description of the indestructible precinct to the visceral mental image of fly-like creatures that explode humans with pelvic appendages and then eat them with the same. This is the basics of how you use structure effectively, and Atreus understands it damn well — which is why this is one of my favourite articles on the site, and why I'm also damn pissed that it's sitting at a meagre +68 at the time I write this.

There's a lot that people can learn from this, and if it helps get this article more visibility on the site then I'm happy to put thirty minutes of my day into writing an analysis like this. This is fucking good horror writing, and I say that as someone who usually hates horror. Re-upvoted with glee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Text logs are hard to read, but I'm more warm on this now than I was nearly two years ago. I can upvote this tentatively I believe.

Sidenote; the tags for this skip seem very sparse. Are three tags really all that can be applied to it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I voted for it, largely because I liked the background detail it filled in about SCP operations in the 50s. I'm honestly not that big a fan of the specific badguys, to the extent we learned much about them. Ultimately, though

BEWARE PELVIC ANOMALY

kind of forced my upvote…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Literally just read that skip before this one. For sure they're the same… except these don't speak English.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah, I was going to mention that too. The "COURAGE BRO" really jumped out at me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a whole lot of nothing here, 's far as I can tell.

I agree

EDIT: I mean I'm all for format screws (cause I have a sick obsession with them), but story wise, I don't really see much here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the gold standard for minor format screws.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted. The text logs were a pain to read, and on the whole, I found them very distracting. There's a whole lot of nothing here, 's far as I can tell. Another scip where the actual content is danced around and never actually addressed, and I don't have the patience for that nor inclination to develop it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Waaait, I remember something like this. Obese humanoids, predatory, large eyes, strange sweat… Is this related to SCP-1788?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great story; the format really lends it verisimilitude, the gaps in the information given to the reader are tantalizing, and the conclusion is very creepy. While the use of "experimental stasis technology" in place of more concrete containment procedures does seem like a bit of a cop-out (excuse the awful pun), the anticipation of the Foundation having to deal with a serious containment problem about eight years from now as a consequence adds to the suspense. +1'ed with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's some really excellent pacing here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite agree with Kalinin: excellent example of style/format screw in service to a story.
Makes you dive directly into the events head first, and the end result is quite impressive.

I also agree with mindcracked: this feels a bit unfinished - like, suspended in time, despite the fact that the actual event took place 20 years ago…

I'm not entirely sure I understand what's (been) going on, but that is not necessarily a bad thing!

Overall, a good skip: +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this in draft and don't think I commented, as I could respect the originality of the medium used, but wasn't quite feeling it.

What clicked for me was realising that this was less about the anomaly (or the magic-tech that's keeping it contained) and more about the momentum of the escalating drama, and the small character touches.

And then remembering that "Bronagh" is of course a female Irish name made both the "COURAGE BRO" and that final message land much more heavily.

+1 - it's a slightly backhanded compliment to say that this is much better written than it may at first appear, but it's testament to the way that the nuts and bolts of the storytelling aren't too obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last note, man. That last note.

I'm also digging the "COURAGE BRO". Solid reminder that these guys are human.

+1 from me.

Edit: Nothing to see here, folks. Move along.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this. The way it's presented is original and very effective imo. They last message from the agent is particularly chilling, given the lack of information. Why had he slipped into shorthand? Is he just exhausted? In shock? Or possibly wounded and struggling to type? Delicious.

I will say that the ending didn't really satisfy me. It feels like there is a great story building, and then someone pulls a magic pause button out of the air and calls it good. It just doesn't feel like the Foundation way of doing things. I know that's vague, though, and no alternate conclusion seems obvious. +1 for DANKE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good delivery with the logs but not feeling it otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So apparently this device is predicted to cease functioning in 2023. Another mention of that in the file might be nice, to point up the fact that the Sealed Evil In a Can isn't likely to remain sealed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is something I would advise others to consult in the sense of when to employ the format screw. The way this is designed is in service to the narrative. Without the real-time transmissions documenting the story of what happened, what you would have is a fairly boring "monster what kills you" article. But with the way this is formatted, the horror of the initial anomaly is conveyed effectively, and what you end up with is a story. The presentation doesn't feel like it could have just been slapped onto anything. And the initial concept demanded a different style of writing.

Another thing this article is useful in demonstrating is that it isn't so much that the site doesn't have room for scary monsters what kill you anymore. I think it's more that it doesn't have room for stories about scary monsters in a box that don't do anything but be scary and occasionally escape because reasons. This is a more dynamic narrative, and the transmissions give it a nice sense of realism.

The thought of the occupants of the building being trapped together with these things in an inescapable time anomaly is a nice way to close this one out. Very chilling. Overall, nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the line isn't feeling bad about itself

Perhaps not; but now I am. I feex, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uhh, hmm, I dunno. I kind of dislike that the anomaly is just "we can't get in the building", and there's nothing else there. All that there is to this skip is "alien gypsies ate everyone blackbox years ago". It's almost an extranormal event, if not for the fact that you anomalously can't enter the building still.

That said, I really like the way this was told, even if the content isn't quite to my liking. You might want to change "insecure" to "unsecure"; the line isn't feeling bad about itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, especially because we don't actually know what the mysterious replicating gypsy anomalies are. It's spooky and the historical aspect definitely plays well here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is different from how most articles are written Ill give it that. I understand what you did here, the building cannot be accessed at this time, so the Foundation does not really know what is going on inside and therefore the desciption is short outside the Incident Log. While the story in the Incident log felt rather simple (and I probably do not understand all of what was going on), I really enjoyed the way it was presented, and thought it was done rather well. +1 from me at least

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These images are my original work, suitable for release under CC-BY-SA.
